title: Field surgeon
prompt: access.log has one record per line: date, time, verdict, user, path. Extract the USER (field 4) of every DENIED line, then produce users.txt: those usernames sorted with duplicates removed. cut -d' ' -f4 slices field 4, or awk '{print $4}' — your choice.

files.contents:
2026-07-03 12:00:01 ALLOWED ty /home
2026-07-03 12:00:04 DENIED mallory /admin
2026-07-03 12:00:09 ALLOWED deploy /api/status
2026-07-03 12:00:12 DENIED eve /admin
2026-07-03 12:00:15 DENIED mallory /etc/passwd
2026-07-03 12:00:19 ALLOWED ty /docs
2026-07-03 12:00:22 DENIED carol /admin
2026-07-03 12:00:30 DENIED eve /backups
2026-07-03 12:00:41 ALLOWED deploy /api/deploy
2026-07-03 12:00:47 DENIED mallory /admin

details.restate: users.txt ends up with the distinct usernames that were DENIED, alphabetical, one per line. Users who only ever got ALLOWED don't appear.

io.because: grep keeps the DENIED records; awk slices out column 4

help.plain: Three steps chained: keep DENIED lines, keep column 4 of them, sort unique into the file.
help.analogy: Reading only the RETURNED column of a delivery ledger, and only for the failed deliveries.
help.mnemonic: grep | awk | sort -u — filter rows, then columns, then tidy.
help.nudge: You've done sort -u before. What two commands get just the usernames of just the DENIED lines in front of it?
